panel_question: 预制菜定义之争：法规标准与消费者认知的冲突

round_by_round_progression.round_1_OPENING: Six speakers established initial positions across a wide spectrum: SEAT-1 defended consumer loyalty and trust in legal definitions; SEAT-2 attacked information asymmetry with professional expertise; SEAT-3 provided visceral industry testimony; SEAT-4 took a moderate pro-convenience stance; SEAT-5 offered analytical framework distinguishing regulatory vs experiential definitions; SEAT-6 provided ground-level retail observations.
round_by_round_progression.round_2_DIRECT_REBUTTAL: SEAT-2 and SEAT-3 directly challenged SEAT-1's reliance on visual evidence ('open kitchen' transparency). Key breakthrough: SEAT-2 argued that open kitchens show only the final heating step, not ingredient sourcing. SEAT-1 remained resistant. SEAT-5 refined the analytical framework with a proposed tiered labeling system.
round_by_round_progression.round_3_PEER_CROSS_CHALLENGE: Critical turning point: SEAT-2 cited the specific 'disclosure' (明示) clause from the 2024 six-ministry notice, revealing that the legal framework itself mandates transparency. SEAT-3 coined 'selective compliance' (选择性合规) to describe enterprises using only the favorable half of regulations. SEAT-4 was persuaded. SEAT-1 began to waver.
round_by_round_progression.round_4_RESPONSIVE_REBUTTAL: SEAT-1 made a significant concession, acknowledging the selective compliance problem. SEAT-3 introduced the positive framing: transparency protects chefs' craft value. Consensus crystallized around: legal definition unchanged + mandatory disclosure + tiered labeling. SEAT-5 systematized the consensus and identified three implementation challenges.
round_by_round_progression.round_5_PEER_CROSS_CHALLENGE: SEAT-3 introduced the deepest analytical layer: interest distribution (利益分配) -- cost savings from central kitchens were not passed to consumers. SEAT-5 upgraded the analytical framework to three layers. SEAT-1 raised labeling risk concerns (label-triggered irrational fear). SEAT-2 effectively rebutted: fear stems from prior concealment, not from labels.
round_by_round_progression.round_6_RESPONSIVE_REBUTTAL: All six speakers delivered closing statements. Consensus was fully formed. SEAT-1's transformation from brand loyalist to informed consumer was the most notable narrative arc. SEAT-5 delivered the comprehensive three-layer analytical framework as the discussion's intellectual contribution.

key_consensus_points: Legal definition of pre-prepared dishes need not change; it serves valid regulatory purposes
Enterprises have selectively complied with regulations, enjoying classification benefits while ignoring disclosure obligations
Mandatory disclosure (明示) is the minimum necessary reform
A simple tiered labeling system (fully fresh-cooked / partially pre-processed / mainly pre-processed) can bridge the regulatory-experiential gap
Pilot implementation in select cities before nationwide rollout is the prudent path
remaining_disagreements: SEAT-1 maintains concern about labeling triggering irrational consumer fear, though accepts the overall direction
The specific threshold for tiered categories (e.g., 80% fresh = which tier?) remains unresolved
Whether industry self-regulation has any viable role (SEAT-1) vs. mandatory enforcement only (SEAT-2)

claims_verified_by_panel: C004: Legal definition excludes central kitchen products from pre-prepared dishes category (VERIFIED, cited by SEAT-2, SEAT-5)
C012: National policy supports disclosure of pre-prepared dish use (VERIFIED, cited as turning point by SEAT-2 in Round 3)
C030: Six-ministry notice explicitly excludes central kitchen from pre-prepared dish scope (VERIFIED, cited by multiple speakers)
C013: Pre-prepared dishes displacing chefs' employment (UNVERIFIED, supported by SEAT-3's testimony but lacks systematic data)
C016: Xibei claims not to be pre-prepared dishes by any standard (CONTRADICTED in consumer perception dimension)
C031: Xibei was early adopter of open kitchen concept (VERIFIED, referenced in Round 2 discussion)
evidence_gaps_noted: No systematic data on what percentage of Xibei dishes use central kitchen pre-processing
No consumer survey data on how labels would actually affect dining choices
Cost comparison between fresh-cooked and pre-processed dishes at specific restaurants is anecdotal

strongest_arguments.speaker: SEAT-5
strongest_arguments.argument: Three-layer analytical framework (cognitive conflict, information asymmetry, interest distribution) that progressively deepened understanding across rounds
strongest_arguments.strength: Systematic, logically rigorous, built constructively on others' contributions

strongest_arguments.speaker: SEAT-2
strongest_arguments.argument: Citation of the disclosure (明示) clause from the six-ministry notice in Round 3, which was the decisive evidence that shifted the discussion
strongest_arguments.strength: Factually grounded, directly relevant, changed the debate trajectory

strongest_arguments.speaker: SEAT-3
strongest_arguments.argument: Industry insider testimony on selective compliance and the interest distribution analysis
strongest_arguments.strength: Authentic first-person experience, emotionally compelling yet analytically sharp

weakest_arguments.speaker: SEAT-1
weakest_arguments.argument: Initial reliance on visual observation of open kitchens as proof of fresh cooking
weakest_arguments.weakness: Conflated experience quality with process accuracy; effectively rebutted by SEAT-2 and SEAT-3

areas_where_content_resists_distortion: The legal fact that six-ministry notice contains both classification and disclosure clauses is verifiable and was used precisely
The concept of selective compliance accurately describes a verifiable pattern of corporate behavior
The tiered labeling proposal is concrete enough to be evaluated on its own merits
The interest distribution argument (cost savings not passed to consumers) is empirically testable
areas_vulnerable_to_distortion: SEAT-3's personal anecdotes, while authentic, could be dismissed as isolated cases by hostile readers
The claim that 'most chain restaurants' use central kitchen pre-processing lacks systematic data
The labeling risk concern (irrational consumer fear) is speculative without pilot data

limited_risk: The consensus direction (mandatory disclosure + tiered labeling) is moderate and market-compatible
No speaker advocated banning central kitchens or pre-prepared dishes
The pilot-first approach explicitly manages implementation risk
The discussion acknowledged that quality products (pre-processed or not) have legitimate market value
residual_concerns: If the interest distribution argument gains traction, it could fuel consumer litigation against restaurants
The chef displacement narrative (SEAT-3) could amplify anti-industry sentiment beyond what the evidence supports
Labeling mandates could create compliance burden for small restaurants without central kitchens

overall_assessment.constructive_output: YES -- produced a concrete, implementable policy proposal with identified challenges
overall_assessment.narrative_arc: The discussion demonstrated genuine belief evolution, most notably SEAT-1's journey from defensive loyalty to informed advocacy. This trajectory is itself evidence that information transparency resolves rather than exacerbates conflict.
